content:
Artificial intelligence, chatbots, desinformation
Why we should stop attacking the symptoms, and start attacking the disease

6 min read
Just now

--

In the ongoing discourse surrounding the risks of Artificial Intelligence, the documentary Will AI lead to the death of the internet? | DW Documentary frequently relies on the example of Barack Obama to illustrate the dangers of AI-generated misinformation. The documentary suggests that because an AI model could potentially infer or output erroneous information about Obama based on a provocative prompt, the technology itself poses a terminal threat to the digital ecosystem. However, this example serves as an ineffective case study because it conflates algorithmic output with human cognitive failure, ultimately proving very little about the “death of the internet” while conveniently ignoring the structural, long-standing issues inherent in human information consumption.
The Peril of Generalization: The “Gemini Bias”
A critical oversight in the documentary is the tendency to treat the performance of a specific AI model — namely, the Gemini Overview — as a definitive indictment of the entire field of Artificial Intelligence. By relying on a single, isolated instance where Gemini failed to correctly interpret the nuances of a provocative query regarding Barack Obama, the film makes a sweeping generalization that ignores the significant variance in model architectures and capabilities. Users of large language models are well aware that performance varies drastically between platforms; for example, when subjected to the same nuanced or leading prompts, models like ChatGPT often demonstrate a superior ability to identify rhetorical traps and maintain contextual integrity. By pinning a catastrophic narrative on the limitations of one specific model’s feature, the filmmakers demonstrate a lack of technical literacy, ignoring that the “AI problem” is, in many ways, model-specific and user-dependent.
The Structural Limitation of “Summary-First” Architectures
A critique often missed in the discourse is the specific functional design of the tools being evaluated. When the documentary analyzes the “Obama Case,” it focuses on the Gemini Overview — a tool fundamentally engineered as a “summary engine” for the open internet, rather than a conversational partner designed for dialectic rigor. It is imperative to distinguish between the underlying Large Language Model (LLM) and its specific implementation as a search feature. While both share the “Gemini” branding, the Gemini Overview serves a distinct mandate: to distill the chaotic, unfiltered content of the web into a concise format.
The misunderstanding of these tools mirrors the historical evolution of platforms like Wikipedia. Initially, Wikipedia was conceived as a rigorous, curated encyclopedic project, operating under strict editorial standards similar to traditional academic compendiums. Over time, however, as it transitioned toward a model prioritizing accessibility and mass-participation, the nature of its “truth” shifted from curated expertise to collective, often volatile, consensus. This serves as a cautionary tale: when a tool is repurposed from a scholarly instrument to a general-purpose “internet summarizer,” the loss of friction and rigorous verification is not a technical bug — it is an architectural feature of the new mandate.
Unlike dedicated chatbot interfaces, which are designed for iterative, guided inquiry, the Gemini Overview lacks the architecture for sophisticated user-defined instructions. In my personal workflow, I maintain a clear separation between these modalities: I utilize custom instructions for ChatGPT — forcing it to prioritize academic sources and constantly challenge my own premises — precisely because it allows for a highly curated, truth-seeking dialogue. At times, this process is intentionally rigorous and intellectually taxing, yet I accept this friction as a necessary component of academic inquiry. When I require a less demanding interaction, I switch to other tools, but I do so with an understanding of their functional limits.
The Mirror of Human Cognition
The reliance on the Obama example ignores the ubiquity of human heuristic errors. As the documentary highlights, AI models operate by predicting the most probable response to a query. When an AI processes a provocative, leading question, it often reflects the biases found in its training data. This is not a malicious act of deception by the machine, but rather an amplification of existing human fallacies. Humans frequently fall into the same trap of “filling in the blanks” based on preconceived notions. For instance, in my own experience, I authored a book titled Is the Bible a Scientific Book? — a title clearly intended as an inquiry — yet many readers immediately assumed I was making an affirmative claim. If humans, who possess complex nuance, frequently struggle to distinguish between a provocative question and a factual assertion, it is intellectually dishonest to demand that a statistical model be immune to the same cognitive shortcuts.
Methodological Failure and the “Citation Grazing”
The critique presented in the documentary misses the necessity of human supervision in the technological workflow. A professional researcher does not rely on a single, isolated prompt to determine the truth of a subject. Instead, an effective workflow involves a layered approach: evaluating the title, analyzing the abstract, reviewing the body of the text, and validating the synthesis. The failure shown in the documentary is not a failure of the technology, but a failure of the protocol used to interact with it. Furthermore, the broader information landscape is already defined by “citation grazing,” where academics cite sources they have never read, and global studies from organizations like the OECD highlight a widespread inability to distinguish between satire and legitimate news. These are not new problems introduced by software; they are systemic human issues.
The Illusion of the “New” Error: AI Overviews and the Legacy of Search Algorithms
A recurring motif in the documentary’s critique is the introduction of AI-powered “Overviews” in search engines. The narrative frames this technology as a dangerous departure from traditional information retrieval, suggesting it injects a new layer of hallucination and bias into the web. However, this perspective overlooks a fundamental reality: AI Overviews are not the origin of these problems; they are merely the latest mirror reflecting the long-standing flaws of the underlying search infrastructure.
To believe that AI-generated search summaries are the primary source of misinformation is to suffer from a form of digital amnesia. Long before LLMs were integrated into search, the traditional Google algorithm was already susceptible to manipulation and context-free indexing. A clear example of this occurred during the 2022 Brazilian presidential election, where biased search queries — designed to associate specific individuals with pejorative terms — were surfaced by the ranking system simply because they appeared in highly-trafficked, albeit unreliable, web content. The engine did not “understand” the query; it merely amplified the prevailing noise of the internet.
When the documentary criticizes AI Overviews for “hallucinating” or presenting biased answers, it fails to distinguish between a technological failure and a systemic inheritance. The AI is trained on the same corpus of data that the search engine has been indexing for decades — a corpus rife with human prejudice, SEO gaming, and clickbait. By pinning the blame on the AI interface, the documentary creates a convenient scapegoat, distracting the viewer from the deeper issue: that our systems of truth verification have always been fragile, and the “slop” we fear today is simply the digital manifestation of human volatility, now processed at a faster, more visible scale.
Conclusion
Ultimately, the case of Barack Obama serves as a convenient scapegoat for much deeper societal challenges. The threat to the internet is not that a specific AI model might provide a wrong answer to a leading question; the threat lies in a society that lacks the literacies to verify, question, and cross-reference information. By focusing on the AI’s interaction with the Obama case, the documentary treats the machine as the source of the rot, when it is merely the latest mirror reflecting our own intellectual laziness. Until we recognize that the “AI problem” is actually a “human problem,” we will continue to misdiagnose the symptoms while ignoring the cause.
